description: The basking shark is the world's second-largest fish, reaching up to 12 m (40 ft), and feeds exclusively by passive filter-feeding on zooplankton as it swims slowly with its enormous mouth agape. Found in cold to warm temperate seas worldwide, it undertakes long seasonal migrations following plankton blooms. Despite its massive size, it is harmless to humans.
funFact: A basking shark's mouth can open over 1 m (3.3 ft) wide, and it filters approximately 2,000 tonnes of seawater per hour to collect tiny zooplankton — it must keep swimming continuously to maintain the flow of water through its gill rakers.
habitat: Cold to warm temperate coastal and oceanic waters worldwide. Often seen near the surface in coastal areas during summer plankton blooms; suspected to overwinter in deep offshore waters. Most commonly observed in the North Atlantic and North Pacific.
diet: Feeds exclusively on zooplankton — primarily copepods, fish eggs, small crustaceans, and invertebrate larvae — by passive ram filter-feeding. Makes no active pursuit of prey; its enormous gill rakers strain plankton from the water as it swims open-mouthed.
size: Typically 6–9 m (20–30 ft); max reliably recorded ~12 m (39 ft). The second-largest fish in the ocean, after the whale shark.

vitalSigns.label: Reproductive Rate
vitalSigns.value: ~6 pups per litter; estimated 2.6–3.5 year gestation (one of the longest of any vertebrate)

vitalSigns.label: Age at Maturity
vitalSigns.value: Estimated 12–20 years (females toward the upper end)

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Zooplankton, copepods, fish eggs, and small crustaceans

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Extremely low — very long gestation and small litters

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Gill rakers filter ~2,000 tonnes of water/hour; massive oil-rich liver provides buoyancy; mouth gape exceeds 1 m wide

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Cold to warm temperate seas worldwide; aggregations most common in the N. Atlantic and N. Pacific

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: Surface to ~1,264 m (4,150 ft); primarily epipelagic during feeding season

threats.name: Historical Targeted Hunting
threats.description: Heavily hunted through the mid-20th century for liver oil (used in lubricants, cosmetics, and vitamins), fins, and fishmeal. Fisheries in British Columbia, Norway, and Ireland collapsed by the 1970s–80s.

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: Slow-moving surface behavior makes basking sharks vulnerable to entanglement in pelagic trawls, gill nets, and set gear. High bycatch mortality has been documented throughout the North Atlantic.

threats.name: Slow Recovery
threats.description: Extremely low reproductive rate — with one of the longest gestations of any shark and small litter sizes — means depleted populations recover on a timescale of decades, not years.

threats.name: Vessel Strikes
threats.description: Extended time spent at the surface filter-feeding makes basking sharks vulnerable to boat collisions. Ship strikes are a documented cause of mortality in coastal shipping lanes.

threats.name: Climate Change
threats.description: Dependent on zooplankton blooms in cold, nutrient-rich waters. Ocean warming and acidification threaten plankton productivity and may reduce food availability in core habitats.

actionItems.title: Report Basking Shark Sightings
actionItems.description: Contribute to citizen science by reporting sightings to the Basking Shark Scotland or local Wildlife Trust databases. Sighting records are essential for tracking population recovery and movement.

actionItems.title: Support Marine Protected Areas
actionItems.description: Advocate for MPAs in key aggregation sites such as the Hebrides, Irish Sea, and Bay of Biscay where basking sharks congregate seasonally and are most vulnerable to disturbance and bycatch.

actionItems.title: Reduce Bycatch in Pelagic Fisheries
actionItems.description: Support policies requiring gear modifications and observer coverage in pelagic fisheries operating in basking shark habitat. Encourage fishing industry representatives to adopt voluntary best practices.

actionItems.title: Fund Basking Shark Research
actionItems.description: Support the Shark Trust's basking shark project and organizations using satellite tags to identify migration routes and critical habitat areas needing legal protection.

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Decreasing — historical fisheries caused severe depletion; recovery slow

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Declining — climate-driven reduction in plankton blooms in core habitats

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Uncertain — zooplankton abundance variable; projected decline with warming

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — targeted fisheries mostly ended; bycatch and vessel strikes persist

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Partial — CITES Appendix II; protected in UK, US, EU; enforcement variable

populationTrendMeta.note: No reliable global population estimate exists. Regional fisheries in Norway, British Columbia, Ireland, and Scotland collapsed between the 1960s and 1980s after intensive directed targeting, but global abundance before and after these collapses has never been formally quantified.

preyDeclineRegions.name: N. Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Copepod and zooplankton abundance declining as ocean warming increases thermal stratification and reduces nutrient upwelling in core basking shark habitat.

preyDeclineRegions.name: Mediterranean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Plankton productivity reduced by warming, acidification, and nutrient runoff; historically important aggregation sites now largely unoccupied.

preyDeclineRegions.name: N. Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Seasonal plankton productivity variable and declining in warming coastal zones; California Current productivity declining with reduced upwelling intensity.

preyDeclineRegions.name: S. Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Cold Benguela and Malvinas upwelling systems still produce abundant zooplankton; lower warming pressure at southern latitudes.

preyDeclineRegions.name: Southern Ocean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Cold, nutrient-rich waters still support high zooplankton density; marginal warming beginning at the northern boundary of Southern Ocean habitat.

fishingPressureRegions.name: N. Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: Target of historic directed fisheries in the UK, Ireland, and Norway; directed fishing mostly ended but bycatch in pelagic trawls and vessel strikes in coastal shipping lanes continue.

fishingPressureRegions.name: Mediterranean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Documented bycatch in pelagic trawls and longlines; vessel strike risk elevated near the Strait of Gibraltar and major shipping routes.

fishingPressureRegions.name: N. Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Bycatch in salmon gillnets and squid drift nets documented; vessel strikes near major Pacific shipping routes.

fishingPressureRegions.name: S. Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: Limited directed effort; some incidental catch in pelagic trawls targeting southern bluefin tuna and squid.

fishingPressureRegions.name: Southern Ocean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Low commercial fishing intensity in deeper Southern Ocean waters; marginal bycatch in longlines targeting toothfish.
